ranges.label: Low Rumination
ranges.description: Your rumination levels are within a healthy range. You tend to process negative experiences without getting stuck in repetitive thought patterns, which supports adaptive emotional regulation and mental well-being.
suggestions: Continue using healthy coping strategies like physical activity and social connection
Practice mindfulness to maintain present-focused awareness
Keep a gratitude journal to reinforce positive thinking patterns

ranges.label: Mild Rumination
ranges.description: You show some tendency toward repetitive negative thinking. While occasional rumination is normal, this pattern may increase vulnerability to low mood if left unchecked. Early intervention can prevent escalation.
suggestions: Try thought-stopping techniques when you notice repetitive negative thinking
Schedule ‘worry time’ — limit rumination to a specific 15-minute window daily
Engage in behavioral activation by planning enjoyable activities
Practice mindfulness meditation to develop observing rather than engaging with thoughts

ranges.label: Moderate Rumination
ranges.description: Your score indicates a significant pattern of repetitive negative thinking. Moderate rumination is a well-established risk factor for depression and anxiety, and may be interfering with your problem-solving ability and daily functioning.
suggestions: Consider cognitive-behavioral therapy (CBT) to identify and challenge ruminative thought patterns
Practice rumination-focused CBT techniques such as functional analysis of thinking
Engage in regular physical exercise, which research shows reduces rumination
Try behavioral activation — replace brooding with concrete problem-solving actions
Consider consulting a mental health professional for personalized guidance

ranges.label: High Rumination
ranges.description: Your score reflects pervasive repetitive negative thinking that likely impacts your mood, concentration, and daily functioning. High rumination is strongly associated with depression and anxiety and typically benefits from professional intervention.
suggestions: Seek professional help — a therapist trained in CBT or rumination-focused therapy can provide structured support
Consider metacognitive therapy (MCT), which specifically targets repetitive thinking patterns
Engage in intensive mindfulness-based cognitive therapy (MBCT) to break the cycle of rumination
Prioritize physical activity and social engagement to interrupt ruminative cycles

ranges.label: Low Mindfulness
ranges.description: Your overall mindfulness level is low, suggesting difficulty staying present, observing experiences non-judgmentally, and responding thoughtfully rather than reacting automatically. Developing mindfulness skills can significantly improve emotional regulation and well-being.
suggestions: Start with brief guided mindfulness meditations (5-10 minutes daily) using an app like Headspace or Calm
Practice informal mindfulness during daily activities such as eating or walking
Consider a structured Mindfulness-Based Stress Reduction (MBSR) course
Try body scan exercises to develop greater awareness of physical sensations
Read introductory books on mindfulness such as ‘Full Catastrophe Living’ by Jon Kabat-Zinn

ranges.label: Moderate Mindfulness
ranges.description: You demonstrate moderate mindfulness abilities with room for growth. You may find that some facets of mindfulness come more naturally than others. Targeted practice in your weaker areas can enhance overall present-moment awareness.
suggestions: Review your subscale scores to identify specific facets needing development
Increase your daily meditation practice gradually toward 15-20 minutes
Explore different meditation styles — loving-kindness, body scan, or breath focus — to find what works best
Practice non-judgmental awareness during emotionally challenging moments
Consider joining a mindfulness meditation group for community support

ranges.label: High Mindfulness
ranges.description: Your mindfulness skills are well-developed across multiple facets. You demonstrate strong capacity for present-moment awareness, non-judgmental observation, and mindful responding. These skills serve as protective factors for mental health.
suggestions: Maintain your mindfulness practice to sustain these beneficial skills
Consider deepening your practice through retreat experiences or advanced meditation
Share your mindfulness skills with others — teaching reinforces personal practice

observing.label: Low
observing.description: You may have difficulty noticing internal and external experiences such as bodily sensations, emotions, sounds, and sights. Developing observing skills helps build the foundation for mindful awareness and emotional intelligence.

observing.label: Moderate
observing.description: You show moderate ability to notice and attend to present-moment experiences. Continued practice in sensory awareness exercises can strengthen this foundational mindfulness skill.

observing.label: High
observing.description: You demonstrate strong observing skills, readily noticing internal sensations, emotions, and external stimuli. This awareness provides a solid foundation for all other mindfulness facets.

describing.label: Low
describing.description: You may find it challenging to put your inner experiences into words. Difficulty describing feelings can hinder emotional processing and interpersonal communication. Labeling emotions practice can help develop this skill.

describing.label: Moderate
describing.description: You have a moderate ability to articulate your internal experiences. Building vocabulary for emotions and practicing affect labeling can further develop this communication-enhancing mindfulness facet.

describing.label: High
describing.description: You are skilled at describing your inner experiences with words. This ability enhances emotional processing, self-understanding, and effective communication with others about your internal states.

actaware.label: Low
actaware.description: You frequently operate on autopilot, engaging in activities without full attention. Low acting-with-awareness increases risk of accidents, errors, and missing important experiences. Mindful attention training can help.

actaware.label: Moderate
actaware.description: You show moderate ability to stay present during activities rather than operating on autopilot. Practicing single-tasking and bringing deliberate attention to routine activities can strengthen this facet.

actaware.label: High
actaware.description: You demonstrate strong ability to stay present and attentive during daily activities. Acting with awareness helps you engage fully in experiences and reduces errors from distraction or autopilot behavior.

nonjudge.label: Low
nonjudge.description: You tend to be highly self-critical and judgmental about your thoughts and feelings. This inner criticism can amplify negative emotions and contribute to anxiety and depression. Self-compassion practice can help.

nonjudge.label: Moderate
nonjudge.description: You show moderate ability to accept your thoughts and feelings without judgment. Continuing to practice self-compassion and acceptance can reduce unnecessary emotional suffering and self-criticism.

nonjudge.label: High
nonjudge.description: You demonstrate strong ability to accept your thoughts and feelings without evaluation. This non-judgmental stance reduces emotional reactivity and supports psychological flexibility and resilience.

nonreact.label: Low
nonreact.description: You tend to react strongly and automatically to your inner experiences. Low non-reactivity means emotions may feel overwhelming or controlling. Learning to observe without immediately reacting can build emotional resilience.

nonreact.label: Moderate
nonreact.description: You show moderate ability to let thoughts and feelings come and go without being caught up in them. Continued meditation practice can further develop this equanimity and emotional balance.

nonreact.label: High
nonreact.description: You demonstrate strong ability to observe internal experiences without being carried away by them. This equanimity allows you to respond thoughtfully rather than react impulsively to emotional triggers.

ranges.label: Low Gratitude
ranges.description: Your gratitude level is below average, suggesting difficulty recognizing and appreciating positive aspects of life. Low gratitude is associated with reduced life satisfaction and well-being. Gratitude is a skill that can be cultivated with practice.
suggestions: Start a daily gratitude journal — write down three things you are grateful for each evening
Practice expressing appreciation to others through notes, messages, or verbal thanks
Try a ‘gratitude walk’ — notice and appreciate positive aspects of your surroundings during a daily walk
Reflect on past challenges you have overcome and what you learned from them

ranges.label: Moderate Gratitude
ranges.description: You experience a moderate level of gratitude. You recognize some positive aspects of your life but may not consistently appreciate them. Deepening your gratitude practice can enhance life satisfaction, relationships, and psychological well-being.
suggestions: Expand your gratitude journal to include both large and small daily blessings
Practice savoring positive experiences by pausing to fully appreciate good moments
Write a gratitude letter to someone who has positively impacted your life
Set reminders to pause and notice things you might take for granted
Try gratitude meditation focusing on appreciation for people and experiences in your life

ranges.label: High Gratitude
ranges.description: You demonstrate a strong sense of gratitude, consistently recognizing and appreciating the positive aspects of life. High gratitude is associated with greater happiness, stronger relationships, better sleep, and enhanced resilience to stress.
suggestions: Continue your gratitude practices to maintain this positive orientation
Share your appreciation with others to strengthen social bonds
Mentor others in developing gratitude skills through your example

ranges.label: Low Overall Affect
ranges.description: Your overall emotional engagement appears reduced, with lower levels of both positive and negative affect. This pattern may reflect emotional numbing, disengagement, or a period of low emotional intensity. Exploring activities that promote emotional vitality may be helpful.
suggestions: Engage in activities that have previously brought you joy or excitement
Practice identifying and labeling your emotions throughout the day
Consider whether this reflects a temporary state or a persistent pattern worth exploring with a professional
Try novel experiences to stimulate emotional engagement

ranges.label: Moderate to High Affect
ranges.description: Your emotional engagement is within a typical range. Review your positive and negative affect subscale scores for a more nuanced picture of your emotional balance. The ideal pattern involves high positive affect combined with low negative affect.
suggestions: Review your subscale scores — aim for high positive affect and low negative affect
Practice activities that boost positive emotions such as exercise, socializing, and pursuing hobbies
If negative affect is elevated, consider stress management techniques like deep breathing or progressive relaxation

positive_affect.label: Low
positive_affect.description: Your positive affect is low, indicating reduced experiences of enthusiasm, alertness, and active engagement. Low positive affect is a hallmark of depression and may signal anhedonia — difficulty experiencing pleasure. Consider activities that promote positive emotions.

positive_affect.label: Moderate
positive_affect.description: Your positive affect is moderate. You experience some positive emotions but there is room for growth. Behavioral activation — scheduling enjoyable and meaningful activities — can help boost positive emotional experiences.

positive_affect.label: High
positive_affect.description: Your positive affect is strong, indicating frequent experiences of enthusiasm, interest, and active engagement with life. High positive affect is associated with greater resilience, better social relationships, and improved physical health.

negative_affect.label: Low
negative_affect.description: Your negative affect is low, indicating minimal distressing emotions such as guilt, fear, or hostility. Low negative affect suggests good emotional regulation and is associated with greater life satisfaction and psychological health.

negative_affect.label: Moderate
negative_affect.description: Your negative affect is in the moderate range. Some negative emotions are a normal part of the human experience. Monitor whether stress or specific situations are driving elevated negative feelings.

negative_affect.label: High
negative_affect.description: Your negative affect is elevated, indicating frequent experiences of distressing emotions such as anxiety, anger, guilt, or fear. Persistently high negative affect is associated with psychological distress and warrants professional attention.

ranges.label: Low Self-Compassion
ranges.description: Your self-compassion is quite low, suggesting a tendency toward self-criticism, isolation during difficult times, and over-identification with negative thoughts. Low self-compassion is associated with increased vulnerability to depression, anxiety, and burnout.
suggestions: Practice self-compassion exercises from Kristin Neff’s work — try the self-compassion break meditation
Challenge your inner critic by asking ‘What would I say to a close friend in this situation?’
Explore Compassion-Focused Therapy (CFT) or Mindful Self-Compassion (MSC) programs
Practice self-kindness through physical gestures like placing a hand on your heart during difficult moments
Consider working with a therapist trained in self-compassion approaches

ranges.label: Moderate Self-Compassion
ranges.description: Your self-compassion is at a moderate level. You show some ability to treat yourself kindly during difficulties but may still default to self-criticism under stress. Strengthening self-compassion skills can improve emotional resilience and well-being.
suggestions: Practice loving-kindness meditation to cultivate warmth toward yourself and others
Notice self-critical thoughts and gently reframe them with self-compassion
Remind yourself that suffering and imperfection are shared human experiences
Try journaling about difficult experiences from a compassionate perspective

ranges.label: Above Average Self-Compassion
ranges.description: Your self-compassion is above average, indicating a healthy balance of self-kindness, common humanity, and mindful awareness during difficult times. This capacity helps buffer against stress and supports psychological flexibility.
suggestions: Maintain your self-compassion practices during challenging periods
Share self-compassion techniques with friends, family, or colleagues who might benefit
Continue developing mindfulness skills to support present-moment awareness

ranges.label: High Self-Compassion
ranges.description: Your self-compassion is very strong. You consistently treat yourself with kindness, recognize your struggles as part of the shared human experience, and maintain balanced awareness of your emotions. This is a significant protective factor for mental health.
suggestions: Continue your self-compassion practices to sustain this valuable inner resource
Consider mentoring or teaching others about self-compassion
Use your strong self-compassion foundation to take on new challenges and growth opportunities

ranges.label: Low Empathy
ranges.description: Your overall empathy score is low, suggesting limited tendency to take others’ perspectives, feel concern for others, or share in others’ emotional experiences. While empathy exists on a spectrum, developing empathic skills can improve relationships and social functioning.
suggestions: Practice active listening — focus fully on understanding others’ perspectives before responding
Read fiction or watch films and try to deeply consider characters’ inner experiences
Engage in volunteer work or community service to broaden exposure to diverse life experiences
Consider whether low empathy is impacting your relationships and seek professional guidance if needed

ranges.label: Moderate Empathy
ranges.description: Your empathy is in the moderate range. You show some capacity for understanding and sharing others’ emotional experiences. Review your subscale scores to see which dimensions of empathy are stronger or weaker for you.
suggestions: Review subscale scores to identify empathy dimensions you can strengthen
Practice perspective-taking by actively imagining how others experience situations
Develop emotional vocabulary to better recognize and understand others’ feelings
Engage in conversations where you prioritize understanding over responding

ranges.label: High Empathy
ranges.description: Your empathy is well-developed across multiple dimensions. You readily understand and share others’ emotional experiences. While this is generally beneficial for relationships, ensure you maintain healthy emotional boundaries to prevent empathic burnout.
suggestions: Practice healthy emotional boundaries to prevent compassion fatigue
Use your strong empathy skills to support others while protecting your own well-being
If personal distress subscale is high, learn grounding techniques to manage emotional contagion

perspective_taking.label: Low
perspective_taking.description: Your tendency to spontaneously adopt others’ viewpoints is low. Perspective-taking is a cognitive empathy skill that can be developed through deliberate practice, improving communication and conflict resolution.

perspective_taking.label: Moderate
perspective_taking.description: You show moderate ability to consider others’ points of view. Continuing to practice seeing situations from multiple perspectives can enhance your interpersonal effectiveness and social understanding.

perspective_taking.label: High
perspective_taking.description: You readily adopt others’ psychological perspectives, which supports effective communication, conflict resolution, and relationship quality. This cognitive empathy skill is valuable in both personal and professional contexts.

fantasy.label: Low
fantasy.description: You have a limited tendency to imagine yourself in fictional characters’ situations. While not problematic, engaging more with narratives can stimulate imagination and indirectly enhance empathic understanding.

fantasy.label: Moderate
fantasy.description: You moderately engage with fictional characters’ experiences. This imaginative capacity can serve as a safe practice ground for understanding diverse emotional perspectives and life situations.

fantasy.label: High
fantasy.description: You strongly identify with fictional characters, readily imagining their thoughts and feelings. This rich imaginative capacity can enhance emotional understanding, though very high levels may sometimes blur fiction and reality.

empathic_concern.label: Low
empathic_concern.description: Your tendency to feel warmth, concern, and compassion for others in distress is low. Low empathic concern may affect relationship quality. Volunteering and exposure to others’ experiences can help develop compassionate responding.

empathic_concern.label: Moderate
empathic_concern.description: You show moderate feelings of concern and sympathy for others experiencing difficulties. This other-oriented emotional response is central to prosocial behavior and healthy relationships.

empathic_concern.label: High
empathic_concern.description: You demonstrate strong feelings of warmth and concern for others in distress. High empathic concern motivates helping behavior and strengthens social bonds, and is a key component of compassionate care.

personal_distress.label: Low
personal_distress.description: You experience minimal personal distress when witnessing others’ negative experiences. Low personal distress indicates good emotional regulation and the ability to remain composed while still being empathic.

personal_distress.label: Moderate
personal_distress.description: You experience moderate personal distress in response to others’ difficult situations. Some self-oriented anxiety in empathic situations is normal, but if it interferes with helping behavior, grounding techniques may help.

personal_distress.label: High
personal_distress.description: You experience significant personal distress when witnessing others’ negative experiences. High personal distress can be overwhelming and may actually impair your ability to help others. Developing emotional regulation and grounding skills is recommended.

ranges.label: No Alexithymia
ranges.description: Your score indicates good emotional awareness and the ability to identify, describe, and reflect on your feelings. You appear comfortable with emotional experiences and can effectively communicate your inner states to others.
suggestions: Continue developing your emotional intelligence through self-reflection and mindfulness
Maintain open emotional communication in your relationships
Use your emotional awareness skills to support others who may struggle with identifying feelings

ranges.label: Possible Alexithymia
ranges.description: Your score falls in the borderline range, suggesting some difficulty with emotional awareness. You may sometimes struggle to distinguish between emotions and physical sensations, or find it hard to describe your feelings to others. This pattern is worth monitoring.
suggestions: Practice emotion labeling — try to name your feelings several times throughout the day
Keep an emotion journal to track patterns in how you experience and express feelings
Explore body-based awareness practices like yoga or progressive muscle relaxation to connect physical sensations with emotions
Consider psychoeducation about emotions through books or workshops

ranges.label: Alexithymia
ranges.description: Your score suggests significant difficulty identifying, describing, and processing emotions. Alexithymia can affect relationships, physical health, and psychological well-being. It is associated with higher rates of depression, anxiety, and somatic complaints, and may benefit from professional support.
suggestions: Consider seeking professional help — therapies such as mentalization-based therapy or emotion-focused therapy can help develop emotional awareness
Practice structured emotion identification exercises using emotion wheels or feeling charts
Explore body-awareness practices to develop connections between physical sensations and emotional states
Engage in creative expression (art, music, writing) as alternative channels for emotional processing
Build a support system of trusted people who can help you recognize and process emotional experiences

difficulty_identifying.label: Low
difficulty_identifying.description: You show good ability to identify your emotions and distinguish them from physical sensations. This skill supports effective emotional regulation and helps you respond appropriately to your emotional needs.

difficulty_identifying.label: Moderate
difficulty_identifying.description: You show some difficulty recognizing and identifying your emotions. You may sometimes confuse emotional arousal with physical symptoms, or feel unsure about what emotion you are experiencing.

difficulty_identifying.label: High
difficulty_identifying.description: You have significant difficulty identifying your emotions. This can lead to confusion about internal states, difficulty knowing what you need, and may manifest as unexplained physical complaints. Emotion identification training may be beneficial.

difficulty_describing.label: Low
difficulty_describing.description: You can effectively describe your feelings to others. This skill supports clear emotional communication, helps others understand your needs, and facilitates emotional processing through verbalization.

difficulty_describing.label: Moderate
difficulty_describing.description: You show some difficulty putting your emotions into words. This may lead to miscommunication in relationships or a sense that others don’t fully understand your experiences. Expanding your emotional vocabulary can help.

difficulty_describing.label: High
difficulty_describing.description: You find it quite difficult to describe your emotional experiences to others. This can create barriers in relationships and limit the benefits of social support. Working with a therapist on emotional expression skills is recommended.

externally_oriented.label: Low
externally_oriented.description: You show good orientation toward inner emotional life rather than exclusively focusing on external events. This introspective capacity supports self-reflection, emotional depth, and psychological insight.

externally_oriented.label: Moderate
externally_oriented.description: You show a moderate tendency to focus on external events rather than inner emotional experiences. While practical thinking has its merits, balancing it with emotional reflection can enhance self-understanding.

externally_oriented.label: High
externally_oriented.description: You strongly prefer focusing on external events and practical details rather than internal emotional experiences. This externally oriented thinking style can limit emotional self-awareness and make psychological processing more difficult.

ranges.label: Normal
ranges.description: Your anger reactions are within the normal range. You manage frustration and anger effectively, without significant impairment in your daily functioning or relationships. Maintaining healthy anger management skills is beneficial for long-term well-being.
suggestions: Continue using effective anger management strategies
Practice stress reduction techniques to prevent anger buildup
Maintain open communication in your relationships about frustrations

ranges.label: Mild
ranges.description: You experience mildly elevated anger reactions. While not severely problematic, you may notice occasional difficulty controlling anger intensity or duration. Learning additional anger management techniques can help prevent escalation.
suggestions: Practice the ‘pause and breathe’ technique — take slow deep breaths before responding when angry
Identify your anger triggers and develop specific coping plans for each
Use physical activity as a healthy outlet for frustration and tension
Try cognitive reappraisal — reframe situations to reduce anger intensity

ranges.label: Moderate
ranges.description: Your anger reactions are moderately elevated, suggesting notable difficulty with anger intensity, duration, or expression. This level of anger may be affecting your relationships, work performance, or physical health through chronic stress activation.
suggestions: Consider anger management counseling or a structured anger management program
Practice progressive muscle relaxation to reduce physiological arousal associated with anger
Keep an anger diary to identify patterns, triggers, and early warning signs
Learn assertive communication skills to express needs without aggression
Explore whether underlying issues like stress, depression, or past trauma contribute to anger

ranges.label: Severe
ranges.description: Your anger reactions are significantly elevated, indicating serious difficulty managing anger that is likely impacting your relationships, work, and overall quality of life. Professional intervention is strongly recommended to develop healthier anger regulation strategies.
suggestions: Seek professional help from a therapist specializing in anger management or emotion regulation
Consider cognitive-behavioral therapy (CBT) which has strong evidence for treating anger problems
Develop a safety plan for managing intense anger episodes, including removing yourself from triggering situations
Explore whether medication consultation may be helpful, particularly if anger co-occurs with depression or anxiety
